title: How breathing affects anxiety and stress
citation: Jerath, R., Edry, J.W., Barnes, V.A., & Jerath, V. (2006). Physiology of long pranayamic breathing: Neural respiratory elements may provide a mechanism that explains how slow deep breathing shifts the autonomic nervous system. Medical Hypotheses, 67(3), 566-571.
content:
Breathing is one of the few physiological processes that operates both automatically and under voluntary control. This dual nature makes it a uniquely powerful lever for influencing the autonomic nervous system, the system that governs the body's stress and relaxation responses.

When a person is anxious or under acute stress, the sympathetic nervous system activates what is commonly called the fight-or-flight response. Breathing becomes faster and shallower, heart rate increases, and blood is redirected to the large muscle groups. This is an adaptive response to genuine threat, but it can be triggered or maintained by psychological stressors even when no physical danger is present. Conversely, slow diaphragmatic breathing stimulates the parasympathetic nervous system via the vagus nerve, producing what is sometimes called the rest-and-digest response: heart rate slows, muscle tension decreases, and the physiological markers of stress begin to subside.

The mechanism involves respiratory sinus arrhythmia, the natural variation in heart rate that occurs with each breath cycle. Inhalation slightly speeds the heart; exhalation slightly slows it. When breathing is slow and deep, especially when the exhalation is extended, this variation is amplified in a way that drives up heart rate variability, a measure of the nervous system's flexibility that is associated with better emotional regulation and lower baseline anxiety.

Pranayamic breathing research, including the work of Jerath and colleagues, has helped clarify how slow breathing at rates of around five to six breaths per minute may influence neural respiratory elements and thalamo-cortical activity in ways that reduce physiological arousal. Practical slow breathing techniques, including box breathing and extended-exhalation breathing, draw on these mechanisms to provide an accessible, evidence-informed tool for managing stress and anxiety in everyday life.
